ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM (Vegetable dishes) used as the closest defensible broader category; no specific Dumpukht curry code exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Dumpukht Vegetable Curry weighs approximately 300 g, representing a typical single-serve restaurant portion.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Indian Council of Medical Research, National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); cross-validated with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for constituent vegetables (cauliflower, potato, green peas, corn)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Dumpukht Vegetable Curry as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN published vegetable curry composites (mixed vegetable curry, cooked with oil and spices) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for the constituent vegetables (cauliflower FDC ID 169986, potato boiled FDC ID 170026, green peas cooked FDC ID 170420, sweet corn cooked FDC ID 169998). The input-record values (energy 126.67 kcal, protein 3.33 g, fat 8.33 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g per 100 g) are consistent with this ICMR-NIN composite and are retained. Trans fat 0.07 g/100g is plausible from ghee content and is retained. Potassium estimated at 380 mg/100g from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy weighted average of constituent vegetables. Calcium 26.67 mg/100g, iron 1.0 mg/100g, cholesterol 8.33 mg/100g, and vitamin C 13.33 mg/100g are consistent with ICMR-NIN composite ranges and are retained.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index Research — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for mixed vegetable curry with potato
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dumpukht Vegetable Curry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on University of Sydney GI Database published entries for potato-containing vegetable curries and mixed vegetable dishes (GI range 55–70 reported for mixed vegetable curries with potato as a primary ingredient). Potato is the dominant high-GI ingredient (GI approximately 70–80 for boiled potato per University of Sydney GI Database); cauliflower, green peas, and corn moderate the blended GI downward. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (30 g = 300 g × 10 g/100g) ÷ 100 = 19.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 250 mg/100g (750 mg per 300 g serving); partially offset by estimated potassium ~380 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g serving delivers approximately 750 mg sodium — about one-third of the 2,000 mg daily DASH limit — so individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation, avoid adding condiments, and pair with potassium-rich sides such as dal or leafy greens to support the sodium-potassium balance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated GI 65 (medium-high), glycemic load 19.5 per 300 g serving; potato and corn are high-GI contributors; dietary fibre only 2.33 g/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glycemic load of 19.5 per serving is clinically significant for Type 2 Diabetes management; individuals should request reduced potato and corn portions, increase cauliflower and green peas, pair with a high-protein side such as dal or paneer to slow glucose absorption, and monitor post-meal blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.33 g/100g (approximately 10 g per 300 g serving) from ghee or cream; cholesterol 8.33 mg/100g; trans fat 0.07 g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content of approximately 10 g per serving approaches one-third of the recommended daily limit for cardiovascular disease patients; individuals should request preparation with minimal ghee or cream, prefer yogurt-based gravy, and note that the vegetable base (cauliflower, peas) provides beneficial dietary fibre and antioxidants that partially offset cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (potato, tomato) contain solanine which may aggravate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) provide partial benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshades should consider requesting substitution of potato and tomato with non-nightshade vegetables such as zucchini or carrot, while retaining cauliflower and the turmeric-ginger spice base for their anti-inflammatory curcumin and gingerol content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dumpukht Vegetable Curry is a rich, aromatic Indian vegetable dish prepared using the traditional dum pukht (slow-cooking in a sealed vessel) technique, which concentrates flavours and produces a deeply fragrant, tender result. The dish features a medley of cauliflower, potato, green peas, and corn, sautéed with onion, ginger, garlic, and a blend of whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala, then simmered in a tomato-based gravy enriched with ghee, yogurt, or cream. The texture is soft and saucy, with the vegetables absorbing the spiced gravy. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 127 kcal, 3.3 g protein, 8.3 g fat (3.3 g saturated), 10 g carbohydrate, 2.3 g dietary fibre, and 250 mg sodium. The estimated glycemic index is 65 (medium), with a glycemic load of approximately 19.5 per standard 300 g bowl, making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free, and is commonly served in Indian restaurants across Singapore. Clinically, the sodium content warrants attention for hypertension, the saturated fat from ghee is relevant for cardiovascular disease, and the nightshade ingredients (potato, tomato) may be a concern for rheumatoid arthritis-sensitive individuals. Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) provide beneficial phytochemicals including curcumin and gingerols.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for Dumpukht Vegetable Curry as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN published vegetable curry composites and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for constituent vegetables. The input-record values are consistent with this composite and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for potato-containing vegetable curries. Medium confidence reflects the absence of an exact named-dish match in any approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Dumpukht-style vegetable curry not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a composite dish entry. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this preparation not reported in available approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported for this composite dish in ICMR-NIN vegetable curry entries or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a unified dish; individual vegetable entries exist but a reliable composite value cannot be defensibly assigned without a direct dish-level measurement. Potassium estimated at 380 mg/100g from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy weighted average of constituent vegetables (cauliflower, potato, green peas, corn, tomato). Trans fat 0.07 g/100g is plausible from ghee content and is retained from the input record. No core macro is zero (protein_g 3.33, fat_g 8.33, carbohydrate_g 10.0); zero-macro justification rule does not apply. GI estimated as analogous from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for this dish exists in any approved source. Ontology codes use broader-class matches only; no fabricated codes used. Nutrient source is ICMR-NIN (primary approved source for Indian dishes) cross-validated with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary global source); no forbidden sources used.
